source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: 3D epithelial cell shapes control topology and cell-cell interface areas for contact signaling
Apico-basally elongated cells have extensive lateral contacts that profoundly impact signaling
3D cell topology provides feedback control between mechanical and biochemical signaling
Lateral cell surface signaling is essential to tune spacing between sensory organ precursors
Perturbing cortical stiffness and cell tortuosity modifies SOP spacing
Fine-tuning signaling range via 3D cell topology may be a unified mechanochemical mechanism

typical_pattern: Opening statement of main finding or importance
Speculation or forward-looking statement about dynamics or generality
Closing with broader implications or future directions

likely_priorities: Dynamic characterization of 3D cell-cell contacts over time
Generalizability across multiple tissue types and organisms
Quantitative validation of signaling range predictions
Robustness of computational framework to segmentation errors
likely_attacks: Lack of dynamic imaging data to support claims about contact dynamics
Reliance on snapshots rather than time-resolved measurements
Speculation about mechanisms without direct molecular evidence
Generalization beyond Drosophila wing disc system
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges that 'So far, it has not been possible to characterize dynamic apico-basal intercalations within live tissues over time' and states 'we speculate that lateral cell–cell contacts might be relatively dynamic' and 'we imagine that dynamic changes in 3D neighbors enable cells to explore their neighborhood'. The authors note 'Continuous improvements in imaging and segmentation may soon make it possible to address the question of contact dynamics experimentally'. They also acknowledge limitations by stating their analysis is of 'snapshots' and discuss what 'we expect' rather than what was directly observed. The paper defends generalizability by listing examples across organisms but acknowledges these are observations rather than tested predictions.

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. Claim strength profile reflects the paper's balanced use of strong claims supported by data alongside appropriate hedging for speculative elements. Reviewer preference profile is grounded in the paper's explicit acknowledgment of limitations regarding dynamic imaging and generalizability.
